home.title: Regular expression visualizer
home.description: Visualize regular expressions as railroad diagrams — modern syntax, live preview.

api.regexToSvg: One call from a regex source and optional flags to a standalone, themeable SVG string. Returns null when the regex fails to parse or can never match.
api.parseRegex: Parses the source to a regexpp AST and validates it. The result carries ok, the ast (null on failure) and an issues array covering both syntax errors and semantic problems.
api.lintRegex: Runs only the semantic checks and returns the issues array — never-matching assertions, empty character classes and the like. Empty for a clean regex.
api.explainRegex: Returns a flat list of steps, each with its depth, syntax category, token slice and a plain-language description. Null when the regex is invalid.
api.formatExplain: Renders one ExplainDesc to a string using a message table. Pass a partial table to localize — any key you omit falls back to the built-in English.
api.explainEn: The built-in English message table that formatExplain defaults to. Use it as the base when you only translate part of the table.
api.buildDiagram: Lays out a parsed AST into a positioned railroad-diagram model with coordinates, ready to render.
api.renderToSvg: Renders a diagram model to a self-contained SVG string; pass the flags to show them after the pattern.
api.sourceColors: Returns the syntax category of each source character (or null), for highlighting the raw pattern text.
api.sourceRanges: Returns, per source character, the start and end offsets of the smallest diagram node covering it — used to sync caret and hover with the diagram.
api.toRegexLiteral: Assembles a valid regex literal from a source and flags, escaping slashes and handling the empty pattern.

ref.title: Syntax reference
ref.subtitle: Click any token to insert it at the cursor.

items.set: one of a, b or c
items.range: one letter from a to z
items.negSet: any char except a, b, c

lint.assertionNeverMatches: This assertion can never be satisfied — the regex will never match.

explain.title: Explanation
explain.invalid: Fix the regex to see an explanation.

usage.subtitle: The diagram engine is a standalone npm package.
usage.intro: It parses a regex into an AST, lays it out as a railroad diagram, and renders a self-contained, themeable SVG — plus syntax/semantic validation and a token-by-token plain-language explanation. Framework-agnostic, usable in any JS/TS project.
usage.featureSyntax: Modern syntax: named groups, lookbehind, Unicode properties, v-flag set operations
usage.featureSvg: Self-contained SVG, themeable via CSS variables (incl. dark mode), exportable
usage.featureLint: Parsing validates too: syntax errors and semantic issues (never-matching assertions, empty classes)
usage.featureAgnostic: Framework-agnostic pure functions, zero runtime dependencies
usage.exRenderDesc: Get a self-contained SVG string in one call — drop it into the page or save it to a file.
usage.exValidateDesc: parseRegex returns syntax errors and semantic issues alike (e.g. an assertion that never matches).
usage.exExplainDesc: Get a token-by-token explanation and print it indented by depth.
usage.exLowerDesc: Two steps: lay out the AST into a model, then render it to SVG yourself.
usage.typesIntro: The shapes returned and accepted by the functions above.
usage.localizeIntro: explainRegex gives each line a ready-made English text plus a language-neutral desc. For other languages, call formatExplain(desc, messages) with a translated table — it can be partial, and any key you omit falls back to English. No i18n library required; the English text is itself rendered this way, so the two never drift.
usage.themeIntro: Every rendered SVG is styled with --rr-* CSS custom properties and ships with light-mode fallbacks baked in, so it looks right out of the box. To recolor it or add a dark theme, set the variables on any element that wraps the SVG — you only override what you want to change, and nothing has to be re-rendered.
usage.themePaletteIntro: One color per syntax category, shared by the source band, the syntax reference and the diagram's accents so a token reads the same color everywhere. These are the --rr-syntax-* variables and their light-mode defaults.
usage.themeStructureIntro: Beyond the palette, these variables drive the rail, text and labels — most of what a dark theme needs — plus the source-band hover highlight.

palette.charset: Shorthand character sets and the dot

test.title: Test strings
test.placeholder: One input per line — each line is validated for a match.
